input_text:
Voyager 1 began its voyage from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch occurred sixteen days after its companion, Voyager 2, a slight scheduling difference that might have surprised observers because NASA had planned separate trajectories for the two probes. The mission, which spanned five years, was powered by a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The primary goal of this undertaking was to execute close flybys of both Saturn and Jupiter, capitalizing on a rare planetary alignment that recurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, it successfully transmitted the initial high-resolution imagery revealing the planet's faint ring system and documented active volcanoes on Io, marking the inaugural observation of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 arrived at Saturn, sending back magnificent sights of its rings and intricate views of Titan. Its subsequent trajectory toward Titan carried Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's ecliptic plane, concluding its planetary science segment while simultaneously commencing its venture into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was situated about 6 billion kilometers away. This solitary photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the Sun's weakening radiation, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This particular photograph was the last capture made by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve its dwindling power supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, thereby earning the distinction of being the inaugural human-made object to reach interstellar space. The mission also incorporates the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc compiled by Sagan—which holds greetings in 55 languages, chosen music, and visual materials intended for any civilization it might encounter. Its ongoing operations are sustained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has led to a gradual reduction in the spacecraft's power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated roughly 25 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling at a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, projections suggest that by November 2026, it will be the first probe to traverse a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch took place sixteen days after its partner, Voyager 2, a slight discrepancy that might have been unexpected because NASA had planned distinct paths for the two probes. The mission, which spanned five years, utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for propulsion. The principal objective of this endeavor was to execute close flybys of both Saturn and Jupiter, taking advantage of a rare planetary alignment that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, it successfully relayed the initial high-resolution images that showed the planet's faint ring system and documented active volcanoes on Io, documenting volcanism outside of Earth for the first time. Two years afterward, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached Saturn, transmitting splendid views of its rings and detailed perspectives of Titan. Its subsequent course toward Titan carried Voyager 1 past the solar system's ecliptic plane, thus concluding its planetary science segment while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

Following advice from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 redirected its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was approximately 6 billion kilometers distant. This singular photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the Sun's diminishing radiation, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This particular photograph was the last capture made by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve its dwindling power supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, achieving the distinction of being the initial human-made object to reach interstellar space. The mission also includes the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc compiled by Sagan—containing greetings in 55 languages, selected music, and visuals intended for any civilization it might meet. Its current operations are maintained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators using plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years contributes to the gradual decrease in the spacecraft's power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is located about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, moving at a speed nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, projections indicate that by November 2026, it will be the first probe to cover a distance equivalent to one light-day.
